probiotic_protocol: LGG supplementation 1 month before delivery and 6 months after delivery to reduce infant atopic eczema by 50%.

prebiotic_note: Oligosaccharides may be beneficial in preventing NEC.

description: Asymmetric IUGR; reduced weight but near-normal length and head circumference.
Symmetric IUGR; reduced weight, length, and head circumference.

diagnostic_rule: If child height centile < MPH centile, investigate for endocrine/metabolic causes.

description: Lack of iodine leading to goitre and CNS defects.
Zinc deficiency manifestations.
Selenium deficiency manifestations.
Excessive intake of fluorine (> 2-3 ppm in water).
Reduced ionized calcium leading to tetany or multisystem irritability.

hypokalaemia_signs: Flat T waves/U waves on ECG

description: Vitamin D deficiency leading to failure of bone mineralisation.
treatment: 6 lakh IU Vitamin D (oral or IM)

description: Vitamin C deficiency affecting collagen synthesis.
Niacin deficiency, common in maize eaters.
Thiamine deficiency.
Neurological disorder caused by excessive consumption of Kesari dhal containing BOAA toxin.
prevention: Parboil Lathyrus sativa seeds to remove toxin.

description: Liver damage caused by Aspergillus flavus fungus affecting groundnuts.
Gluten sensitivity leading to malabsorption.
Liver disease linked to excess copper intake and cooking in copper vessels.
Genetic copper overload disorder.
treatment_adjuvant: High zinc supplementation (Zinc competes with Copper for absorption).

description: Reduced small intestinal length causing malabsorption.
clinical_note: Large intestine absorbs short chain fatty acids (SCFA) produced by fermentation, acting as an energy source.

description: Increased intraocular pressure.
supportive_care: Spirulina consumption is traditionally noted to assist in management.

description: Persistent feeling of hunger.
management: Phenylalanine (as in Spirulina) signals the brain to stop hunger pangs.

description: Recovery phase of chronic diarrhea.
dietary_aid: Amylase Rich Food (ARF) to increase calorie density and reduce viscosity.

description: Allergic skin condition.
probiotic_aid: LGG supplementation shown to reduce incidence by 50% in children with family history.

description: Viral infectious diarrhoea in children.
probiotic_treatment: LGG reduces duration of hospitalization and increases IgA-secreting cells.
preventive: Supplemented formula (B. bifidus + S. thermophilus) reduces shedding (10% vs 39%).

description: Antibiotic-associated bacterial infection.
biotherapeutic_agent: Saccharomyces boulardii (yeast) reduces toxin production and relapses.

description: Inflammatory bowel disease.
probiotic_maintenance: Non-pathogenic E. coli or VSL#3 (combination of 8 strains) maintains remission.

description: Inflammatory bowel disease.
turbo_probiotics: Genetically engineered Lactococcus lactis secreting IL-10 used for treatment.

description: Gastric bacterial infection leading to ulcers.
probiotic_antagonist: L. salivarius and L. acidophilus improve eradication rates (87% vs 70%).

description: Supportive care for CF respiratory health.
support: LGG supplementation reduces incidence of severe respiratory infections.

description: Severe lack of protein and/or calories in children.
consequences: Low BAEP and nerve conduction velocity

description: Oedematous protein-energy malnutrition; disease of the 'deposed child'.

oedema_grading.Grade III: Grade II + paraspinal and chest oedema

clinical_signs.recovery_sign: Tick Sign (initial weight loss followed by gain)

description: Non-oedematous protein-energy malnutrition characterized by extreme wasting.
signs: Baggy pants appearance (loose skin at buttocks)

wasting_grading.Grade IV: Buccal pad of fat (last to disappear)

description: Chronic PEM leading to stunting without acute wasting.
Severe undernutrition in young infants due to abrupt stoppage of breastfeeding.
cluster_findings: Weaning to ragi/bottle too early (< 4 months)
notes: Often occurs after a diarrhoeal episode where breastfeeding is wrongly stopped.

description: Mild to moderate malnutrition not immediately obvious.
Tremors seen during recovery/treatment phase of PEM.
Severe secondary immunodeficiency caused by PEM, comparable to HIV/AIDS.
Neurological degeneration in PEM characterized by anterior horn cell damage.
Functional isolation seen in malnourished children.
Heart dysfunction linked to specific nutrient gaps in PEM.
Apparent worsening during nutritional rehabilitation of severe PEM.
Metabolic disturbance that occurs during the reinstitution of nutrition to patients who are starved or severely malnourished.

definition: Excess adipose tissue; BMI > 30 kg/m2 or > 20% above ideal weight.
tracking_risk: 80% of obese children become obese adults.

description: A cluster of conditions including insulin resistance, dyslipidaemia, and hypertension linked to central obesity.
Impaired glucose uptake in skeletal muscle due to elevated plasma FFA levels.
mechanism: Impaired GLUT4 translocation to the cell membrane.
consequence: Hyperinsulinaemia and potential progression to Type 2 Diabetes.

description: Syndromic obesity due to paternal chromosome 15 deficiency/microdeletion.
Foetal overgrowth syndrome with excess IGF-II (Chromosome 11).
Autosomal recessive obesity syndrome.
Most common type; due to excessive calorie intake.
features: Disproportionately small external genitalia (embedded in fat)

description: Endocrine obesity characterized by short stature and central fat distribution.
Most common micronutrient deficiency; impacts IQ (9-point reduction) and work efficiency.
neurological_impact: Decreased dopaminergic receptors, increased opiate receptors.

clinical_indicators: Night blindness (> 1% prevalence signifies public health problem)

description: Toxicity due to excessive intake of A and D fish oil pearls.

stages.covert_compensated: BP normal, thirst, drowsiness, nausea. Splanchnic circulation sacrificed for systemic.
stages.overt_compensated: Tachycardia, wide pulse pressure, cold extremities, systolic BP maintained.
stages.decompensated_shock: Low BP, tachycardia changes to bradycardia, myocardial hypoxia, urine < 1 ml/kg/hour.

emergency_intervention: Bolus 10-20 ml/kg NS or RL; repeat as needed (max 8 rations).

clinical_grades_children.moderate: 7-10% weight loss, drowsy, rapid pulse, sunken eyes.
clinical_grades_children.severe: 10-15% weight loss, comatose, feeble pulse, BP < 90 mmHg, skin retracts > 2s.

sodium_types.hypertonic: Serum Na > 150 mEq/L (CNS hemorrhage risk; correct slowly @ 10 mEq/L/day)

description: Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH secretion common in head injury or pneumonia.
fluid_management: Restrict fluid to 2/3 maintenance if oedema or hyponatraemia present.

description: Liver dysfunction and bile flow obstruction occurring after prolonged parenteral nutrition (usually > 3 weeks).
Major infectious complication of central venous access in parenteral nutrition.
prevention: Strict asepsis, laminar flow hood preparation, daily set changes.

description: Increase in frequency, fluidity, and volume of stool; usually self-limiting (4-7 days).

pathogenesis.osmotic: Villus cell damage leading to reduced absorption (e.g., rotavirus, disaccharidase deficiency). Stops on fasting.
pathogenesis.secretory: Enterotoxins (e.g., cholera, ETEC) stimulate crypt cell secretion. Does not stop on fasting.
pathogenesis.invasive: Cytotoxin-mediated inflammation producing blood and mucus (Dysentery).

description: Diarrhoea lasting more than 14 days.

clinical_markers.steatorrhoea: Suggested by > 6-8 droplets per LPF on Sudan Red stain; suggests fat malabsorption.
clinical_markers.prolapse_rectum: Often seen in Whip Worm (WW) colitis.

pathophysiology: Elevated ammonia levels due to liver failure.

description: Occurs when > 20% of the intestine is resected.

adaptation_phases.stage_2: Up to 6 months: Reduction in diarrhoea and adaptation.

description: Insulin-dependent, often 'brittle' in children.
Extreme form of obesity with cardiorespiratory disease.
Deficiency of phenylalanine hydroxylase or THBP cofactor.
odour: Musty or mousy odour (phenylacetic acid) in urine.
management: Low phenylalanine diet (25-50 mg/kg), maintain serum PA 3-15 mg%.

description: Cystathionine synthetase deficiency leading to accumulated homocystine.
management: Low methionine diet, large doses of B6 (200-1000mg), B12, and Folic acid.

description: Defect in branched chain keto acid dehydrogenase.
odour: Sweet odour of maple syrup or burnt sugar in urine/body fluids.
management: Low branched-chain amino acid diet (Valine, Leucine, Isoleucine), high-dose thiamine cofactor.

description: Deficiency in galactose-1-phosphate uridyl transferase or galactokinase.
dietary_management: Absolute avoidance of lactose and galactose. Use Soya isolate formulas (Nusobee, Zerolac); avoid whole soya flour.

subtypes.isovaleric: Sweaty feet odour in urine; use glycine (250 mg/kg).
subtypes.multiple_carboxylase: Tom cat urine smell; use biotin (10 mg/day).
subtypes.propionic_methylmalonic: Ketotic hyperglycinaemia; use B12, Biotin, L-carnitine.

description: Abnormal concentration or structure of glycogen.
management: Frequent feedings, continuous night NG feeding, uncooked corn starch for slow absorption.

description: Nausea, vomiting, and collapse due to preformed heat-resistant enterotoxins (A-E).
Lethal toxin blocking acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction.
Histamine response due to ingestion of spoiled fish (Tuna, Mackerel, Mahi-mahi).
Immediate IgE-mediated hypersensitivity reaction.
Intermediate Arthus-type immune complex reaction (IgG mediated).
Non-immunological adverse reaction to food components.
The 'Barker Hypothesis' proposing that foetal undernutrition leads to permanent metabolic programming and adult CVD/Diabetes.

indicators.low_ponderal_index: Stronger predictor of CHD than birth weight.
indicators.small_baby_syndrome: Consistent relation to insulin resistance and hypertension.

description: Psychological eating disorders common in adolescent dieters seeking to remain slim.
Appearance of secondary sexual characteristics before the expected age.
Prolonged inadequate energy intake leading to low BMI, common in adolescents and prospective mothers.
Common skin condition in adolescence influenced by hormones and diet.
Progressive loss of skeletal muscle mass and strength associated with aging, replaced by fat.
effects: Increased risk of falls and fractures
management: Resistance exercise and adequate protein intake.

description: Vitamin B12 deficiency common in the elderly due to hypochlorhydria (reduced gastric acid).
Difficulty in swallowing resulting from stroke, Alzheimer's, or physiological changes.
Skin breakdown caused by prolonged pressure, common in bedridden or institutionalized elderly.
stages: Stage I to Stage IV (increasing severity)

description: Lack of salivation (dry mouth) affecting over 70% of the elderly.
Critical clinical evaluation for infants 1 week to 2 months.
Fever evaluation based on endemic risk (High/Low).
clinical_signs: Fever (temp >= 37.5 C or hot to touch)
Fever present every day for > 7 days

description: Triage categories for post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP).

categories.Class_I: Licks on intact skin, touching animal. Treatment: None (if history reliable).
categories.Class_II: Minor scratches, no bleeding, licks on broken skin. Treatment: Immediate vaccination.
categories.Class_III: Multiple bites, bleeding wounds, mucosal contamination. Treatment: RIG + Vaccination.

description: Most common helminth infection (Ascaris lumbricoides), transmitted faecal-orally.
management: Piperazine or Pyrantel pamoate; deworming mandatory prior to elective surgery.

description: Intestinal parasite sucking up to 0.2 ml blood per worm per day.
Passage of live adult dung beetles per rectum, common in unclothed children.
mechanism: Beetles crawl up through the anus due to faecal smell and warmth; they do not develop in the gut.
management: Warm soap and water enema; prevention of reinfection.

description: Chronic infection (Robert Koch, 1882) leading to a vicious cycle with malnutrition.
management: DOTS (Directly Observed Treatment Short-course) using HRZE drugs.

description: Tuberculosis resistant to at least INH and Rifampicin.
management: DOTS-Plus (Category IV) using 6 drugs (Kanamycin, Ofloxacin, Ethionamide, etc.) for 18-24 months.

description: Critical electrolyte imbalance (low sodium) caused by over-consumption of water relative to exertion/loss, common in recreational exercisers.
Condition where small pouches (diverticula) form in the intestinal wall, often managed by a high-fiber diet.
nutritional_management: Increased intake of insoluble and soluble fiber (20-35 g/day) to prevent constipation and add stool bulk.

description: Spectrum of heat illness caused by severe dehydration and thermoregulatory failure.
Mechanical damage to red blood cells in the feet due to running on hard surfaces with poor footwear.
consequence: Iron loss leading to athletic anaemia/deficiency.

description: Total exhaustion of muscle/liver glycogen stores (typically after 2 hours of moderate-high intensity exercise), leading to sudden fatigue and performance collapse.
management: Refueling with easily digestible carbohydrates during exercise (30-60g/hr) and high-CHO recovery meals.

description: Accelerated weight/height gain in infants (especially SGA) intended to correct early growth faltering.
long_term_risks: Obesity in childhood and later life
Adverse effects on body composition and puberty

description: Premature increase in BMI following the nadir (usually at 5-6 years), associated with metabolic risks.
Delay in motor, adaptive, language, and social domains caused by early-life multideprivation.
management: Composite stimulation package (Nutritional input + Developmental stimulation + Psychosocial support).

description: Failure to achieve 80% brain volume by age 2 due to chronic malnutrition.
Normal developmental phase occurring around 6 months where the infant shows distress towards unfamiliar faces.
clinical_notes: Peak occurs between 6-9 months; signifies healthy attachment to primary caretakers.

description: Deficit in cognitive ability measured by IQ, categorized into levels of functional capability.
Adverse effects from high omega-6 to omega-3 ratios (e.g., > 5:1), common in sunflower/safflower oils.
management: Shift to oils with medium chain triglycerides (MCTs) like coconut oil to achieve ratio < 5:1.

description: Behavioral state in severely malnourished children where they withdraw from the environment and fail to interact or seek stimulation.
management: Composite Stimulation Package (STIM) to re-engage the child with the environment.
